task: The user wants help choosing between an Electron app and an `aiki serve` web interface, refining the concept, selecting standout features, and planning its development for a useful and impressive hackathon submission. AND ALSO: The user wants to decide whether to build a local web server ('aiki serve') or an Electron app to provide a stunning graphical interface for their Aiki CLI multi-LLM orchestration tool for an upcoming hackathon.
constraints: Aiki is currently a CLI tool.
The application must orchestrate installed Claude Code, Codex, and Gemini/Antigravity CLIs.
The product should remain local-first and require no API keys.
Users should be able to conduct research from a visual interface and receive a synthesized final output.
The result should impress hackathon judges while remaining genuinely useful.
Must leverage the existing aiki-cli tool
Must have premium UI/UX to impress judges
Must be genuinely useful for developers
unknowns: Hackathon judging criteria, rules, required technologies, submission format, and deadline.
Available implementation time, team size, and technical strengths.
Evidence from existing users showing which Aiki workflow provides the strongest value.
Compatibility and security requirements for invoking local provider CLIs from a visual application.
Expected demo environment and whether judges can install all required provider CLIs.
Specific hackathon tracks, themes, or judging criteria (namastedev.com/hackathon access was blocked)
success_criteria: Select either Electron or `aiki serve` with a defensible rationale.
Improve and validate the proposed product concept.
Define standout features that demonstrate Aiki's unique value.
Explain the end-to-end user and orchestration workflow.
Provide a practical development plan suitable for the hackathon.
Selection of the best architecture (serve vs electron)

domain_dimensions.label: Local CLI execution boundary
domain_dimensions.rationale: Electron and a browser-based local server expose installed model processes differently, affecting security, permissions, streaming, cancellation, and reliability.

domain_dimensions.label: Hackathon Wow Factor
domain_dimensions.rationale: Visualizing complex multi-agent orchestration dynamically (e.g., showing Claude and Gemini debating in real-time) can heavily influence hackathon judging.

domain_dimensions.label: Council observability
domain_dimensions.rationale: The interface must make parallel model reasoning, disagreement, judging, and synthesis understandable or the product may look like an ordinary chat wrapper.

domain_dimensions.label: Distribution Friction
domain_dimensions.rationale: Adding 'aiki serve' to an existing npm CLI is frictionless for current users, whereas an Electron app requires a separate, heavy download.

domain_dimensions.label: Demo-environment portability
domain_dimensions.rationale: The verdict changes if judges cannot install or authenticate every provider CLI needed for a live demonstration.

alternatives: Local web application launched by `aiki serve`
success_bar: A working, polished demonstration that makes multi-model orchestration visually understandable, delivers a clearly better synthesized result, is easy to run, and has credible value beyond the hackathon.
evidence_supplied: The published `aiki-cli` package is at version 0.3.2.
Aiki is described as a local-first AI model council for code review and idea stress-testing.
The package currently uses Claude Code, Codex, and Gemini/Antigravity CLIs without API keys.
The hackathon page was supplied but could not be fetched because automated access returned HTTP 403.
It orchestrates installed LLM CLIs (Claude Code, Gemini/Antigravity, Codex) locally without API keys
missing_evidence: Hackathon judging criteria, rules, required technologies, submission format, and deadline.
Available implementation time, team size, and technical strengths.
Evidence from existing users showing which Aiki workflow provides the strongest value.
Compatibility and security requirements for invoking local provider CLIs from a visual application.
Expected demo environment and whether judges can install all required provider CLIs.
Specific hackathon tracks, themes, or judging criteria (namastedev.com/hackathon access was blocked)
